testcase: Azurestackhci Typespec SDK Missing Release Date
query:
title: Azurestackhci Typespec SDK Missing Release Date

question: My Release pipeline failed with 
 
debug]Verifying as a release build because ForRelease parameter is set to true
##[error]Entry has no release date set. Please ensure to set a release date with format 'yyyy-MM-dd'. See https://aka.ms/azsdk/guideline/changelogs for more info.

[Pipelines - Run 20260412.1 logs](https://dev.azure.com/azure-sdk/internal/_build/results?buildId=6142385&view=logs&j=39ab1972-b17d-5c84-f843-9f3c82ed353f&t=699b90fe-8f25-5aa6-005f-5f09941176d0&l=40)
 
[azure-sdk-for-net/sdk/azurestackhci/Azure.ResourceManager.Hci/CHANGELOG.md at main · Azure/azure-sd…](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/azurestackhci/Azure.ResourceManager.Hci/CHANGELOG.md)
 
is missing release date.
 
It just says, 1.3.0-beta.1 (Unreleased). 
 
Please help me fix this
ground_truth:
Yes — this release build is failing because the latest changelog entry is still marked `(Unreleased)`, and the verifier also found empty sections.
What to change
In `Azure.ResourceManager.Hci/CHANGELOG.md`, replace:
`## 1.3.0-beta.1 (Unreleased)`
with:
`## 1.3.0-beta.1 (2026-04-13)`
Use your actual intended release date in `yyyy-MM-dd` format.
Also remove any empty sections. Your current entry has an empty ### Bugs Fixed section. So make it:
```
## 1.3.0-beta.1 (2026-04-13)
### Features Added
- Upgraded API version to `2026-04-01-preview`
- Migrated from Swagger/AutoRest to TypeSpec-based generation
- Added support for new resource types and operations from the latest API version
### Breaking Changes
- This version is a major migration from AutoRest to TypeSpec-based code generation.
- Some property types changed (for example, `ConnectivityProperties` moved from `BinaryData` to a typed model).
- Some resource collection and extension method signatures changed.
- Some model factory overloads changed signatures.
### Other Changes
- Backward-compatible type aliases are preserved but may be removed in a future release.
```
Important
The same pipeline run also shows `Azure.ResourceManager.Hci.Vm/CHANGELOG.md` has the same problem: `Unreleased` plus empty sections. Fix that file too before rerunning.
For .NET release validation, changelog date updates often need to be done manually before the release run.
Next steps
Update both changelog files in https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net.
Push the PR.
Rerun the failed release job from https://dev.azure.com/azure-sdk/internal/_build/results?buildId=6142385&view=results.
If you want, I can draft the exact corrected changelog entry for `Azure.ResourceManager.Hci.Vm` too.

testcase: Should SDKs be named after the Product or the RP
query:
title: Should SDKs be named after the Product or the RP

question: We have a product that uses our RP AzureLocal. According to the APEX criteria, RPs are required to have an SDK. The product, AKS Everywhere, does not expose any RP.
For the APEX criteria, is it enough for AzureLocal to have its own SDK, or should the product that uses AzureLocal RP as its interface develop a wrapper SDK?
ground_truth:
Based on the documented requirements, APEX (CPEX) SDK compliance is evaluated at the REST API surface (management-plane/data-plane) level, not at every individual product/feature that is layered on top of an existing RP.
If AKS Everywhere does not expose its own REST API/RP and instead relies solely on the existing AzureLocal RP as its API surface, then the CPEX SDK requirement applies to the AzureLocal management-plane APIs. In that case:
- AzureLocal must meet the SDK requirements (Tier 1 SDKs, release plan, API readiness + SDK release milestones, etc.).
- AKS Everywhere would not be required to ship an additional “wrapper SDK” on top of AzureLocal purely for APEX compliance, because it does not introduce a separate public API surface.

If, in the future, AKS Everywhere exposes its own RP or additional REST APIs (management or data plane), those APIs would then need to satisfy the same SDK requirements and have their own SDK coverage or an approved exception.

testcase: How to run pipelines on Python, Go and Java SDK Merge
query:
title: How to run pipelines on Python, Go and Java SDK Merge

question: once SDK PRs are merged for Python, Go and Java SDK, how do I run release pipeline
ground_truth: You can release a package using the Azure SDK tools agent from vscode or copilot-cli. Open vscode or copilot-cli from azure-rst-api-specs or any of the Azure SDK language repo root and then you can use a prompt like "Release <package name> in <language>". Agent will identify the pipeline and initiate a release pipeline run. You will need monitor the pipeline run and approve the release stage of your SDK once pipeline completes the build stage.

query:
title: Can't find generated PRs

question: Hi all, I have this [GitHub issue](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/issues/42524) tracking SDK releases for Compute, however I cannot find the link to the generated PRs anywhere in the pipeline even though the issue indicates that these PRs have been created. Where can I find those? Or is there something that went wrong?
ground_truth:
It should show up in the spec PR as bot comment. If not, there should be failure message.
Please note these PRs are only for SDK testing purpose. If to proceed with SDK release, ensure to trigger SDK PRs with a release plan.

testcase: Release Plan Dashboard Not Reflecting Released SDK Package Status
query:
title: Release Plan Dashboard Not Reflecting Released SDK Package Status

question: I linked a different JS SDK PR to the release plan because another package with the same API version had already been merged and released last week: Azure SDK Release Plan Dashboard. Released package: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@azure/arm-msi/v/2.3.0-beta.1, which has the same package version of the linked PR: [[AutoPR @azure-arm-msi]-generated-from-SDK Generation - JS-6319430 by azure-sdk · Pull Request #385….](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/pull/38589/changes#diff-7098e92859fe28791786c71aa37c3576a7e12e68f746a327e0e3f821a1a693c7)
However, the “Package Link” and “Action Required” fields on the dashboard are not correctly reflected after updating the PR. How does the dashboard determine whether a package has already been released or not?
ground_truth:
Engineering systems already has automation to close any stale PRs if inactive for a specific period. It is not supported now to automatically close a PR when a release plan is abandoned.
 
Release planner dashboard does not use the PR content to identify the package version. Release status and release version is set in the release plan by release pipeline by looking up a release plan for the package name. JavaScript package @azure/arm-msi was released on 5/20 and release pipeline did not find any matching release plan for the package. Package name was updated in the release plan later on 5/21. 
 
release status is updated by the release pipeline and it won't recheck the status if there was not release plan was found during the release stage.
 
I will mark this package as Released now.
